questions.prompt: Test scores have mean 70 and standard deviation 8. A score of 86 has a z-score of:
A negative z-score means the observation is:
Scoring at the 90th percentile on an exam means:
choices: 90% of test-takers scored at or below your score
Q1, Q2, Q3, mean, standard deviation

questions.prompt: A dataset has Q1 = 40 and Q3 = 60. Using the 1.5×IQR rule, a value of 95 is:
choices: not an outlier, because 95 < Q3 + IQR
not an outlier, because outliers must be below Q1
impossible to judge without the mean

questions.prompt: Two students compare standardized test results: one has z = +1.15 on test A, the other z = +1.13 on test B (different scales). The z-scores let us conclude:
choices: the first student answered more questions correctly
the two stand in nearly the same position relative to their own test populations
test A is harder than test B
nothing — z-scores from different tests can never be compared

questions.prompt: ACT scores have mean 21, SD 5.2; SAT scores have mean 1060, SD 195. One student scored 29 on the ACT, another 1350 on the SAT. Who performed better relative to their test population? Show the comparison.
questions.modelAnswer: The ACT student's z-score is (29 − 21)/5.2 ≈ 1.54; the SAT student's is (1350 − 1060)/195 ≈ 1.49. Both are strong, but the ACT student stands slightly higher relative to their own test's population, so the ACT student performed marginally better in relative terms.

rubric.description: Concludes via the z-scores that the ACT performance is (slightly) stronger relative to its population

questions.prompt: A hospital growth chart says an infant's weight is at the 15th percentile. A worried parent reads this as a failing grade of 15%. Explain what the number actually means and why it is not a grade.
questions.modelAnswer: The 15th percentile means the infant weighs more than about 15% of infants the same age and less than about 85% — it is a position within the distribution of healthy infants, not a score out of 100. Percentiles rank; they don't grade. A perfectly healthy child must occupy every percentile, including the 15th; the number only becomes concerning in context, such as a sudden drop across percentiles over time.

rubric.description: Explains percentile as position relative to peers (15% at or below)
Distinguishes rank from score/grade (someone must be at every percentile)

questions.prompt: For the sorted dataset 55, 62, 68, 70, 72, 75, 78, 80, 84, 88, 92, 98 (n = 12), find the five-number summary and state whether a new value of 20 would be flagged as an outlier by the 1.5×IQR rule.
questions.modelAnswer: Minimum 55, Q1 = 69, median = 76.5, Q3 = 86, maximum 98. The IQR is 86 − 69 = 17, so the lower fence is 69 − 1.5(17) = 43.5. A value of 20 falls below 43.5, so it would be flagged as a suspected outlier.

rubric.description: Computes the lower fence (43.5) and flags 20 as an outlier

questions.prompt: Your team dashboards report the p95 latency of an API. Connect this to percentile language from the lesson: what does p95 = 480ms claim, and why might a team track p95 instead of the mean latency?
questions.modelAnswer: p95 = 480ms means 95% of requests complete in 480ms or less — the 95th percentile of the latency distribution. Teams track it because latency data is right-skewed: a few very slow requests inflate the mean, while p95 directly describes the experience of nearly all users and is resistant to those extreme values.

rubric.description: States that 95% of requests are at or below 480ms
Explains skew/outlier inflation of the mean and percentile resistance (tail experience)
